title: What is a coroutine?
description: What are coroutines in Python?
options: Functions defined with async def that can be paused (await) and resumed; they run in an event loop and are the building blocks of asyncio concurrency
correct_answer: Functions defined with async def that can be paused (await) and resumed; they run in an event loop and are the building blocks of asyncio concurrency

title: What is the difference between a module and a package?
description: How do modules and packages differ?
options: A module is a single .py file; a package is a directory containing __init__.py and multiple modules, creating a namespace hierarchy
correct_answer: A module is a single .py file; a package is a directory containing __init__.py and multiple modules, creating a namespace hierarchy

title: What is the functools module?
description: Name useful functions from functools.
options: lru_cache, wraps, partial, reduce, total_ordering, singledispatch, cached_property — tools for working with higher-order functions
correct_answer: lru_cache, wraps, partial, reduce, total_ordering, singledispatch, cached_property — tools for working with higher-order functions

title: What is @functools.lru_cache?
description: What does lru_cache do?
options: A decorator that caches function results using a Least Recently Used strategy with configurable maxsize; dramatically speeds up functions with repeated arguments (e.g., recursive Fibonacci)
correct_answer: A decorator that caches function results using a Least Recently Used strategy with configurable maxsize; dramatically speeds up functions with repeated arguments (e.g., recursive Fibonacci)

title: What is duck typing?
description: What does 'duck typing' mean in Python?
options: If it walks like a duck and quacks like a duck, it's a duck — Python checks behavior (methods/attributes) rather than explicit type; enables polymorphism without inheritance
correct_answer: If it walks like a duck and quacks like a duck, it's a duck — Python checks behavior (methods/attributes) rather than explicit type; enables polymorphism without inheritance

title: What is the ABC module?
description: What does the abc module provide?
options: Abstract Base Classes: ABC and @abstractmethod force subclasses to implement specific methods; provides a formal way to define interfaces in Python
correct_answer: Abstract Base Classes: ABC and @abstractmethod force subclasses to implement specific methods; provides a formal way to define interfaces in Python

title: What is the __enter__ and __exit__ protocol?
description: How does the context manager protocol work?
options: __enter__ runs when entering 'with' block (returns value for 'as'); __exit__ runs when leaving (handles cleanup + optionally suppresses exceptions by returning True)
correct_answer: __enter__ runs when entering 'with' block (returns value for 'as'); __exit__ runs when leaving (handles cleanup + optionally suppresses exceptions by returning True)

title: What is the @contextmanager decorator?
description: What does contextlib.contextmanager do?
options: Converts a generator function with a single yield into a context manager; code before yield = __enter__, code after yield = __exit__
correct_answer: Converts a generator function with a single yield into a context manager; code before yield = __enter__, code after yield = __exit__

title: What is the pathlib module?
description: Why use pathlib over os.path?
options: Object-oriented filesystem paths: Path objects with / operator for joining, methods like .read_text(), .exists(), .glob(); more readable than os.path string manipulation
correct_answer: Object-oriented filesystem paths: Path objects with / operator for joining, methods like .read_text(), .exists(), .glob(); more readable than os.path string manipulation

title: What is a generator expression?
description: How does a generator expression differ from a list comprehension?
options: Uses () instead of []: (x**2 for x in range(10)); produces values lazily one at a time instead of creating the entire list in memory
correct_answer: Uses () instead of []: (x**2 for x in range(10)); produces values lazily one at a time instead of creating the entire list in memory

title: What is the walrus operator in comprehensions?
description: How can := be used in comprehensions?
options: Assign and use a value in one expression: [y for x in data if (y := expensive(x)) > threshold] — avoids calling expensive() twice
correct_answer: Assign and use a value in one expression: [y for x in data if (y := expensive(x)) > threshold] — avoids calling expensive() twice

title: What is the typing module?
description: What does the typing module provide?
options: Type hints for complex types: List, Dict, Optional, Union, Tuple, Callable, TypeVar, Generic, Protocol, Literal, TypeAlias, TypeGuard
correct_answer: Type hints for complex types: List, Dict, Optional, Union, Tuple, Callable, TypeVar, Generic, Protocol, Literal, TypeAlias, TypeGuard

title: What is Protocol in typing?
description: What does typing.Protocol enable?
options: Structural subtyping (static duck typing): defines an interface that classes satisfy implicitly by having the right methods, without explicit inheritance
correct_answer: Structural subtyping (static duck typing): defines an interface that classes satisfy implicitly by having the right methods, without explicit inheritance

title: What is the __all__ variable?
description: What does __all__ do in a module?
options: A list of names that should be exported when 'from module import *' is used; controls the public API of a module
correct_answer: A list of names that should be exported when 'from module import *' is used; controls the public API of a module

title: What are f-strings?
description: What are formatted string literals?
options: f'text {expression}' — embed expressions directly in strings (Python 3.6+); support format specs, conversions (!r, !s), and even = for debugging (f'{x=}')
correct_answer: f'text {expression}' — embed expressions directly in strings (Python 3.6+); support format specs, conversions (!r, !s), and even = for debugging (f'{x=}')

title: What is the difference between copy() and deepcopy()?
description: When do you need deepcopy?
options: copy() creates a shallow copy (nested objects are shared references); deepcopy() recursively copies all nested objects. Need deepcopy when modifying nested data shouldn't affect the original
correct_answer: copy() creates a shallow copy (nested objects are shared references); deepcopy() recursively copies all nested objects. Need deepcopy when modifying nested data shouldn't affect the original

title: Implement async web scraper.
description: Write a simple async function to fetch multiple URLs concurrently.
correct_answer:
import asyncio
import aiohttp

async def fetch(session, url):
    async with session.get(url) as response:
        return await response.text()

async def fetch_all(urls):
    async with aiohttp.ClientSession() as session:
        tasks = [fetch(session, url) for url in urls]
        return await asyncio.gather(*tasks)

# results = asyncio.run(fetch_all(['https://example.com', 'https://example.org']))

title: What is the __getattr__ method?
description: When is __getattr__ called?
options: Called when normal attribute lookup fails (attribute not found); allows dynamic attribute handling. __getattribute__ is called for every attribute access
correct_answer: Called when normal attribute lookup fails (attribute not found); allows dynamic attribute handling. __getattribute__ is called for every attribute access

title: What is the difference between classmethod and regular method?
description: How does classmethod work with inheritance?
options: classmethod receives the actual class as first argument (cls), so it works correctly with inheritance (cls is the subclass when called on a subclass)
correct_answer: classmethod receives the actual class as first argument (cls), so it works correctly with inheritance (cls is the subclass when called on a subclass)

title: What is the bisect module?
description: What does the bisect module provide?
options: Binary search functions for sorted lists: bisect_left, bisect_right for finding insertion points, insort_left, insort_right for inserting while maintaining sort order
correct_answer: Binary search functions for sorted lists: bisect_left, bisect_right for finding insertion points, insort_left, insort_right for inserting while maintaining sort order

title: What is the heapq module?
description: What does heapq provide?
options: Min-heap operations on a regular list: heappush, heappop, heapify, nlargest, nsmallest; Python only has min-heap (negate values for max-heap)
correct_answer: Min-heap operations on a regular list: heappush, heappop, heapify, nlargest, nsmallest; Python only has min-heap (negate values for max-heap)

title: What is the match statement for error handling?
description: Can match/case be used for exception handling patterns?
options: match/case can match exception types and attributes in except* groups (Python 3.11+ ExceptionGroup); also useful for matching error response patterns in general
correct_answer: match/case can match exception types and attributes in except* groups (Python 3.11+ ExceptionGroup); also useful for matching error response patterns in general

title: What is namedtuple?
description: What does collections.namedtuple provide?
options: Creates tuple subclasses with named fields: Point = namedtuple('Point', ['x', 'y']); immutable, memory-efficient, accessible by name or index
correct_answer: Creates tuple subclasses with named fields: Point = namedtuple('Point', ['x', 'y']); immutable, memory-efficient, accessible by name or index
